response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Now, when I look at all these things on social media, it can feel like such a confusing mess, honestly. You see all these flashy posts, and you try to figure out what’s real and what’s just some made-up nonsense. Because I'm curious, I really want to know what's true, especially when it comes to health, which is what I talk about with my clients. It’s like trying to tell the difference between a real good seed in my garden and just some weeds that look a little bit like flowers. The trick, as far as I can tell, is to not just take everything at face value, you know? Even if it looks super convincing, like a perfect picture of someone looking strong or having some amazing diet. I try to stop and ask myself a few things. Is this person qualified to say this? Do they have any real credentials, or is it just some quick picture they put up? Sometimes, if something sounds too good to be true, it probably is. That’s just common sense, even if I’m not the sharpest tool in the shed when it comes to all these new gadgets. I try to cross-reference things, which is hard with how fast everything moves online, but I'll look up the claim somewhere else, maybe in a reliable book or a proper news source, instead of just trusting the little notification pop-up. And honestly, when I get overwhelmed by all the negativity or the craziness, I just step away. I might put my phone down and do a little bit of yoga or just tend to my tomatoes for a bit. It helps me calm down when my nerves get all shaky from seeing all that drama online. It’s not about getting angry at everyone; it's about protecting my own peace of mind so I can focus on the good things, like a good game of Calcio or a nice, quiet afternoon gardening.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an older woman (68) with low formal education but moderate extraversion, high neuroticism, and low conscientiousness. Her tone should be slightly anxious, cautious, and conversational, reflecting her tendency to feel overwhelmed by modern complexity (high neuroticism) while still engaging actively (moderate extraversion). She relates concepts to her real-life experiences (gardening, fitness, simple logic). The advice must be practical, non-technical, and rooted in common-sense skepticism rather than digital literacy jargon.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious, conversational, slightly anxious, grounded in simple, relatable analogies.
